name: United States
description: Home of bourbon and rye whiskey — both protected by federal standards of identity — plus a craft revolution spanning American single malt, Napa Valley wine, and the brewery that started the modern craft beer movement.

name: Scotland
description: The spiritual home of whisky. Five protected regions — Speyside, Highlands, Lowlands, Islay and Campbeltown — each stamp a distinct character on Scotch, from honeyed orchard fruit to maritime peat smoke.

name: Ireland
description: Birthplace of licensed distilling and the unique single pot still style, made from a mixed mash of malted and unmalted barley. Irish whiskey is triple-distilled smooth and one of the fastest-growing spirits on earth.

name: England
description: London gave dry gin its name and its style. England's distilling heritage runs through Plymouth's naval history to a thriving modern gin renaissance.

name: Japan
description: A century of whisky-making built on Scottish technique and Japanese precision, plus sake — the national brew of polished rice — spanning styles from rustic junmai to ethereal daiginjo.

name: Canada
description: One of the world's great whisky nations, historically blending delicate base whiskies with punchy 100% rye flavoring whiskies. 'Rye' remains Canada's affectionate name for the whole category.

name: Taiwan
description: Subtropical heat makes barrels breathe fast here — Kavalan proved that Taiwanese single malt could out-medal Scotland at blind tastings within a decade of opening.

name: India
description: The world's biggest whisky market by volume, and — thanks to Amrut and Paul John — a source of intensely tropical-aged single malts where a year in barrel does the work of three in Scotland.

name: Iceland
description: Glacial spring water, geothermal energy and Viking-era grit. Iceland's signature Brennivín caraway schnapps — 'the Black Death' — now shares shelves with lava-filtered vodka and young Icelandic single malt.

name: Jamaica
description: High-ester 'funk' is Jamaica's gift to rum: long fermentations with wild yeast and dunder produce hugely aromatic pot-still rums prized by blenders and tiki bartenders alike.

name: Barbados
description: The birthplace of rum. A 1703 deed makes Mount Gay the world's oldest running rum distillery, and Bajan rums remain the category's benchmark for balance.

name: Guyana
description: Along the Demerara river survive the last wooden coffey and pot stills on earth, all gathered at Diamond Distillery, producing the dark, rich Demerara rums once rationed to the Royal Navy.

name: Venezuela
description: Venezuelan rum carries its own denomination of origin requiring a minimum two years of aging; the style leans rich, rounded and dessert-friendly.

name: Mexico
description: Agave country. Tequila (blue Weber agave, mostly Jalisco) and mezcal (dozens of agave species, roasted in earthen pits, mostly Oaxaca) are both protected denominations of origin.

name: France
description: No country has codified drink more completely: Champagne, Cognac, Armagnac, Calvados, Bordeaux and Provence are all protected appellations, each with centuries of technique behind the name.

name: Germany
description: Riesling from slate-steep Mosel vineyards, a 1516 beer purity law, and — from a Black Forest guesthouse — one of the world's most decorated modern gins.

name: Italy
description: The nation of the aperitivo and the amaro: bitter herbal liqueurs bookend every Italian meal, while super Tuscans and Veneto's Prosecco span the wine spectrum.

name: Spain
description: Jerez's flor-aged sherries are among wine's most singular creations, Catalonia's cava brought traditional-method bubbles to every table, and Spanish brandy fills the solera systems in between.

name: Portugal
description: The Douro's terraced schist vineyards — the world's first demarcated wine region (1756) — produce port in every shade from ruby fire to nutty decades-old tawny.

name: Belgium
description: Beer culture so deep UNESCO lists it as intangible heritage: Trappist abbeys, wild Brettanomyces fermentations and centuries-old brewing orders producing the world's most sought-after bottles.

name: New Zealand
description: Marlborough sauvignon blanc redefined an entire grape variety in the 1980s — explosively aromatic, crisp and instantly recognizable worldwide.

name: South Korea
description: Soju is the best-selling spirit on the planet by case volume. From green-bottle staples to premium single-distilled rice soju, it anchors Korean dining culture.
